coredata.dc:title: Versatile Tb3+–carbon-dot nanoplatform for fluorescence detection and in situ inactivation of bacterial spores

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xinxin
Sun, Shan
Cheng, Ke
Lin, Tao
Wang, Henggang
Sun, Haoyi
Lin, Hengwei
Wang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Pathogenic bacterial spores pose a considerable threat to human health due to their ability to survive extreme environmental conditions. Thus, sensitive detection and efficient inactivation of these spores are essential for preventing disease transmission. However, most existing studies have predominantly concentrated on detection alone. Herein, we developed a versatile nanoplatform that integrates sensitive bacterial spore sensing and in situ spore inactivation capabilities by assembling terbium ions (Tb3+) with photosensitizing carbon dots (CDs), referred to as Tb-CDs nanoassemblies (NAs). By leveraging the unique optical properties of CDs and Tb3+ ions, along with the antenna effect of lanthanides, Tb-CDs NAs demonstrated highly sensitive visual detection of the key bacterial spore biomarker, pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), achieving a low detection limit of 0.68 μM and excellent selectivity. Additionally, Tb-CDs NAs exhibited practical sensing capabilities in various environments, including spore suspensions and real samples, while monitoring bacterial spore germination. Further studies confirmed that the DPA-triggered disassembly of Tb-CDs NAs led to the release of CDs, enabling higher affinity for spores and in situ inactivation through singlet oxygen generation. This work underscores the potential of CDs-based assemblies for simultaneous pathogen detection and inactivation, providing promising tools for preventing bacterial spread and contamination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Terbium ions
Fluorescence detection
In situ inactivation
Bacterial spores

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of Tb-CDs NAs
Detection of DPA at various scenarios and possible mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of Tb-CDs NAs. (a) TEM image of CDs (inset: particle size distribution and HR-TEM image). (b) TEM image and (c) HRTEM image of Tb-CDs NAs. (d) FT-IR spectra, (e) Zeta potentials and (f) UV–vis absorption spectra and FL emission spectra of CDs and Tb-CDs NAs.

DPA sensing at solution level. (a) FL spectra of Tb-CDs NAs solutions with varying concentrations of DPA. (b) FL intensity of Tb-CDs NAs solutions at 545 nm with increasing DPA concentrations. (c) Linear correlation between DPA concentrations (0–1.4 μM) and the corresponding FL intensity of Tb-CDs NAs solutions. (d) Changes in FL intensity of Tb-CDs NAs solutions at 545 nm with the addition of potential interferers. (Inset: photos of samples under UV light at 275 nm, F and F
0 represent the FL intensity of Tb-CDs NAs with and without the addition of DPA, respectively.).

DPA sensing at complicated scenarios and possible mechanism. (a) Schematic illustration of the bacterial spore germination process highlighting DPA release and the sensing procedure for real samples. (b) FL spectra of Tb-CDs NAs solutions with the addition of bacterial spores at different concentrations. (c) FL spectra of Tb-CDs NAs solutions with the addition of pretreated real samples incubated with bacterial spores at varying concentrations. (d) TEM images of Tb-CDs NAs after addition of DPA. (inset: HR-TEM image) (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of Tb-CDs NAs solutions before and after addition of DPA. (Inset: photos of samples under UV light at 275 nm.).

Imaging of bacterial spores germination. (a) Schematic illustration of the imaging process for bacterial spore germination and the underling mechanism. (b) CLSM images of Bacillus subtilis spores germinated for varying durations followed by the addition of Tb-CDs NAs (the merged field above and the FL field below with the excitation at 405 nm).

In situ activation of bacteria spores. Time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) CDs, (b) Tb-CDs NAs and (c) DPA added Tb-CDs NAs dispersions in the presence of ABDA under continuous 660 nm laser irradiation (52 mW/cm2). (d) Absorbance decays of ABDA at 378 nm in the presence of CDs, Tb-CDs NAs and DPA added Tb-CDs NAs. (e) Plate photographs of bacillus subtilis spores treated with Tb-CDs NAs at various concentrations followed by exposure to 660 nm laser (477 mW/cm2) for different times and, f) corresponding statistical histograms of the antibacterial efficiency.
Schematic illustration of (a) the preparation of Tb-CDs NAs and (b) their applications for FL detection and in situ inactivation of bacterial spores.

head.ce:title: Versatile Tb3+–carbon-dot nanoplatform for fluorescence detection and in situ inactivation of bacterial spores

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Joint Research Center for Photo-responsive Molecules and Materials School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Joint Research Center for Photo-responsive Molecules and Materials School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Healthcare Co., Ltd., Rugao, Jiangsu 226599, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic bacterial spores pose a considerable threat to human health due to their ability to survive extreme environmental conditions. Thus, sensitive detection and efficient inactivation of these spores are essential for preventing disease transmission. However, most existing studies have predominantly concentrated on detection alone. Herein, we developed a versatile nanoplatform that integrates sensitive bacterial spore sensing and in situ spore inactivation capabilities by assembling terbium ions (Tb3+) with photosensitizing carbon dots (CDs), referred to as Tb-CDs nanoassemblies (NAs). By leveraging the unique optical properties of CDs and Tb3+ ions, along with the antenna effect of lanthanides, Tb-CDs NAs demonstrated highly sensitive visual detection of the key bacterial spore biomarker, pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), achieving a low detection limit of 0.68 μM and excellent selectivity. Additionally, Tb-CDs NAs exhibited practical sensing capabilities in various environments, including spore suspensions and real samples, while monitoring bacterial spore germination. Further studies confirmed that the DPA-triggered disassembly of Tb-CDs NAs led to the release of CDs, enabling higher affinity for spores and in situ inactivation through singlet oxygen generation. This work underscores the potential of CDs-based assemblies for simultaneous pathogen detection and inactivation, providing promising tools for preventing bacterial spread and contamination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A versatile nanoplatform that integrates sensitive bacterial spore sensing and in situ spore inactivation capabilities was developed by assembling terbium ions with photosensitizing carbon dots for preventing bacterial spread and contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacterial spores present a considerable threat to both homeland security and healthcare due to their unique structural properties [1,2]. Encased in a robust protective coat, these spores can endure extreme environmental conditions, such as high temperatures, pressure, and UV radiation, allowing them to remain dormant for extended periods [3,4]. In particular, anthrax spores have garnered considerable attention due to their potential to cause severe harm and high mortality rates, even making them a focus for biological weapons development [5,6]. To mitigate potential threats, great efforts have been directed toward developing effective methods for the detection of bacterial spores. Previous studies have identified 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) as a major component of bacterial spores, accounting for 5–15 % of their dry mass, and it is widely regarded as a key biomarker for spore detection [6–9]. Additionally, the germination of bacterial spores is accompanied by the release of DPA [10], making it feasible to monitor the spore germination process by detecting fluctuations in DPA levels.

Various techniques, such as polymerase chain reaction, immunoassays, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy and high-pressure liquid chromatography have been explored for DPA detection [11–15]. However, these methods often require complex instrumentation, lengthy testing times, expensive reagents, and laborious analysis procedures [16]. Among these reported methods, fluorescence (FL) detection has gained favor due to its high sensitivity, rapid response, and low cost [17,18]. Recently, lanthanide-containing FL sensors have attracted considerable attention for their unique properties, including large Stokes shifts, high quantum yields, narrow emission bands, and, notably, the antenna effect [19–21]. The antenna effect enables the efficient sensitization of lanthanide ion FL by DPA through coordination with its carboxyl and nitrogen atoms [22]. These features make lanthanide-containing materials ideal FL sensors for DPA detection.

To reduce the risk of bacterial infection and prevent disease transmission, both the detection and inactivation of bacterial spores are essential. Therefore, developing a multifunctional platform that enables sensitive DPA detection and simultaneous in situ spore inactivation is urgently needed but remains challenging to achieve. Carbon dots (CDs), as small-sized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, are known for their versatile physicochemical properties, including tunable optical characteristics, photo-induced heat and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation capacities [23,24]. These features make CDs ideal candidates for sensing and light-triggered therapeutic applications [25–28]. Additionally, the abundance of surface functional groups on CDs provides active sites for coordinating with metal ions, such as lanthanide ions, further expanding their functionality [29]. Therefore, the combination of CDs with lanthanide ions presents a promising approach for developing multifunctional platforms capable of both DPA sensing and spore sterilization.

In this study, a versatile nanoplatform (Tb-CDs NAs) was developed for both visual DPA detection and in situ spore sterilization, by assembling terbium ions (Tb3+) with photosensitizing CDs ( Scheme 1). This simple assembly process imparts the nanoplatform with several remarkable features: i) due to the unique optical properties of both Tb3+ ions and CDs, Tb-CDs NAs demonstrate highly sensitive visual detection of DPA, with a low detection limit of 0.68 μM and excellent selectivity; ii) as a FL probe for bacterial spore detection, Tb-CDs NAs have shown practical applications across various settings, including spore suspensions and real samples, and were even effective in monitoring bacterial spore germination; iii) the strong coordination affinity between DPA and Tb3+ ions leads to DPA-triggered disassembly of the Tb-CDs NAs, resulting in the release of CDs, providing a clear sensing mechanism; iv) the robust ROS generation ability of CDs further enables efficient in situ sterilization, as confirmed by antibacterial studies. These combined features position Tb-CDs NAs as a promising FL platform for sensitive detection and inactivation of bacterial spores.

Anhydrous CaCl2, anhydrous MgCl2, NaCl, phthalic acid (o-PA), benzoic acid (BA), 2-pyridinecarboxylic acid (2-PA), L-lysine (L-Lys), D-glutamic acid (D-Glu), D-alanine (D-Ala), L-alanine (L-Ala), n-acetyl-d-glucosamine (NAG), methanol, copper chloride dihydrate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, anhydrous sodium sulfate, 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC), beef extract, and tryptone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), polyethyleneimine (PEI), glutathione (GSH), formamide, MnCl4·4H2O, ammonium chloride were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). FeCl2·4H2O, 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene) dipropanedioic acid (ABDA), MnSO4·H2O were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co. Dodecylamine, Tb(NO3)3·6H2O were purchased from Beijing Yingnuo Kaisheng Technology Co. Lecithin-Tween 80 nutrient agar was purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbial Technology Co. Bacillus subtilis strain ATCC6633 III was purchased from Shanghai Baozheng Microbiology Co. The reagents mentioned above were used as received without any further treatment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were captured using a JEM-2100PLUS microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on a SHIMADZU UV-2600 spectrophotometer, while fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured with a Hitachi FS5 spectrophotometer. Zeta potentials were measured using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern). The photodynamic performance of the CDs or Tb-CDs NAs was evaluated under irradiation from a 660 nm laser (Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co. Ltd., China). Bacterial spore images were captured using a laser confocal scanning microscope (CLSM) (TCS-SP8).

CDs were prepared using a solvent-thermal method. Briefly, 2.83 g of GSH and 1.76 g of PEI were dissolved in 50 mL of formamide and loaded into an autoclave, which was then heated in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was diluted with 150 mL of deionized water and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles. The solution was purified by dialysis against water for one week (MWCO 3500 Da). Finally, the CDs powder was collected by lyophilization and stored at 4 °C.

Tb-CDs NAs were synthesized through the simple assembly of CDs and Tb3+ ions, with the concentrations of Tb3+ ions and CDs screened and optimized to be 10 mM and 500 μg mL−1, respectively. Briefly, 2.5 mL of Tb(NO3)3·6H2O solution (40 mM) was slowly added into 7.5 mL of CDs solution (0.667 mg mL−1). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred to centrifuge tubes, and the precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. After washing twice with ultrapure water, the final product was redispersed in water for further use.

Bacillus subtilis strain ATCC 6633 III was utilized in this study. A small loop of Bacillus subtilis spores was collected with a disposable pick-up ring and inoculated into 200 mL of liquid medium. The culture was incubated at 37 °C in a shaking incubator at 200 rpm for 3 days. Following incubation, the cultures were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with water. The pellets were then resuspended in 100 mL of re-suspension medium (RM) and incubated at 37 °C in the shaking incubator for an additional 3 days. After this period, the cultures were again centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, washed three times with deionized water, and the final cultures were resuspended in 5 mL of saline. This suspension was stored at 4 °C for later use.

To evaluate the sensing performance of Tb-CDs NAs, varying volumes (0–80 μL) of DPA (500 μM) were added to 40 μL of Tb-CDs NAs solution (2.5 mg/mL) and diluted to 2 mL with deionized water. The mixtures were allowed to react for 2 min, after which the FL emission spectra were recorded with excitation at 272 nm. For assessing the anti-interference properties of Tb-CDs NAs, 40 μL of CaCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, o-PA, BA, 2-PA, L-Lys, D-Glu, D-Ala, L-Ala, and NAG (500 μM) were added to an aqueous solution containing the same amount of Tb-CDs NAs, followed by the same FL measurement.

To induce bacterial spore germination, 100 μL of L-Ala (40 mM) was mixed with three times the volume of spore suspension and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm. The spores were then diluted with saline to achieve an optical density (O.D.) 600 of approximately 0.5 (∼ 1 × 109 spores/mL). Various volumes (0–187 μL) of the pretreated spore suspensions were immediately mixed with 40 μL of Tb-CDs NAs (2.5 mg/mL) and diluted to 2 mL with deionized water. The sensing procedure followed was identical to that described above.

Briefly, 500 mg of rice were placed in a 30 mL reagent bottle, and 2 mL of ungerminated spore suspension was added, followed by dilution to 20 mL with saline. After incubation at 37 °C in a shaking incubator at 200 rpm for 48 h, L-Ala (10 mM) was added to induce bacterial spore germination. The mixture was then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was collected and diluted with saline to achieve an O.D. 600 of approximately 0.5. Various volumes (0–400 μL) of the pretreated samples were mixed with 40 μL of Tb-CDs NAs (2.5 mg/mL) and diluted to 2 mL with deionized water. The sensing procedure was identical to that described above.

Briefly, L-Ala (10 mM) was added to 10 mL of spore suspension and incubated at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm for 0, 5, 10, 20, and 30 min, respectively. The mixture was then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, with the supernatant collected and diluted with saline to achieve an O.D. 600 of approximately 0.5. Samples were directly used for imaging via CLSM.

The generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) in an aqueous solution was assessed using ABDA as an indicator. 2 mL of CDs (50 μg/mL), Tb-CDs NAs (1 mg/mL), and a mixture of Tb-CDs NAs (1 mg/mL) with DPA (20 μM) were placed in cuvettes, with ABDA added subsequently. These cuvettes were then exposed to a 660 nm laser at power densities of 52 and 477 mW/cm2 for 30 min. The UV absorption of ABDA was measured at various intervals during irradiation to monitor changes over time.

The antibacterial activity of Tb-CDs NAs was evaluated using the plate count method. 200 µL of L-Ala (10 mM)-treated bacterial spore suspension (1 × 106 spores/mL) were mixed with various concentrations of Tb-CDs NAs solution (0, 150, 300, 600, and 800 µg/mL) and incubated for 5 min. Samples in the laser treatment group were then exposed to 660 nm laser irradiation (477 mW/cm2) for 0, 10, 20, and 30 min, respectively. Following irradiation, 100 µL of the suspension was spread on agar plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The bacterial colonies on the plates were subsequently quantified and photographed. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Tb-CDs NAs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of Tb-CDs NAs is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1a, with detailed procedures provided in the Experimental methods section. CDs with excellent water solubility and unique 1O2 generation properties were synthesized following our previous report [30]. Tb3+ ions were utilized as a “glue” for assembly and also served as a fluorescent indicator for DPA detection. Notably, the reactant ratio, a critical factor in the assembly process, was optimized and set at a 1:3.2 mass ratio of Tb3+ ions to CDs, based on their response to DPA (Fig. S1). The successful synthesis of Tb-CDs NAs was initially confirmed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1a, well-dispersed quasi-sphere particles with mean diameter of 2.54 nm were revealed. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images displayed lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, indicative of graphite-like structures. However, striped nanostructures were observed in the TEM image of Tb-CDs NAs (Fig. 1b), demonstrating the successful formation of nanoassemblies with a distinct morphology. The yield of the Tb-CDs NAs was calculated to be approximately 25 %. The HR-TEM results further confirmed the existence of CDs in Tb-CDs NAs (Fig. 1c), as evidenced by the identical lattice spacing to that of free CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on both CDs and Tb-CDs NAs to further examine the changes in crystallinity following assembly. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the XRD spectrum of CDs features a broad diffraction peak centered at 22°, indicating a disordered arrangement of carbon atoms, which is characteristic of their small particle size. In contrast, the XRD spectrum of Tb-CDs NAs exhibits distinct diffraction peaks between 20° and 30°, confirming again the successful assembly of CDs and Tb3+ ions and indicating an improvement in crystallinity. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Zeta potential measurements were subsequently performed to investigate the chemical composition and surface conditions of Tb-CDs NAs. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the CDs exhibited distinct peaks at 1400 and 1660 cm−1, along with a broad peak between 3200 and 3500 cm−1, corresponding to C–N, CO, and O-H/N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. These peaks indicate the presence of amino and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which provide ample coordination sites for assembling with Tb3+ ions. The FT-IR spectrum of Tb-CDs NAs displayed similar characteristic peaks to those of CDs, reflecting their dominant portion in the nanoassemblies. The content of Tb3+ ions was determined to be approximately 12.56 % by mass, as measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). According to the XPS analysis results shown in Fig. S3, the XPS spectra of CDs display characteristic peaks corresponding to C (284.9 eV), O (531.9 eV), N (399.7 eV), and S (162.9 eV). High-resolution (HR) XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p further confirm the presence of chemical bonds such as CO, C-O/C-O-C, N-CO, C-NC, thiophene, and thiolate, which are consistent with the FT-IR analysis. As anticipated, the Zeta potential shifted positively from − 19.38 mV for free CDs to − 10.43 mV for Tb-CDs NAs, attributed to the positively charged Tb3+ ions (Fig. 1e). Finally, the optical properties, including UV-Vis absorption and FL emission, were evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 1f, the characteristic absorption peaks of CDs at around 425 and 640 nm showed a noticeable reduction in intensity in the spectrum of Tb-CDs NAs, likely due to coordination with Tb3+ ions. Despite this, both CDs and Tb-CDs NAs showed a maximum FL emission peak at approximately 680 nm under optimal excitation at 405 nm, indicating that the FL properties of the CDs were largely preserved after assembly. Additionally, the FL emission behavior of Tb-CDs NAs under optimal excitation of the Tb3+ ions channel at 275 nm was examined. As shown in Fig. S4, the characteristic peaks of Tb3+ ions were barely detectable without the addition of DPA due to the absence of the antenna effect, while the emission peak attributed to the CDs remained present. Additionally, the recorded excitation spectra of Tb-CDs NAs primarily reflected the properties of the CDs (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA at various scenarios and possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing capacity of Tb-CDs NAs for DPA was systematically investigated across various scenarios. Initially, the sensing behavior of Tb-CDs NAs in aqueous solution was examined. As shown in Fig. 2a, a significant increase in FL emission intensity at 489 nm, 545 nm, 562 nm, and 621 nm was observed with rising DPA concentrations, attributed to the 5D4-7FJ (J = 6, 5, 4, and 3) transitions of Tb3+ ions [7]. Interestingly, a moderate increase in FL emission at 680 nm (maximum enchantment of ∼ 30 %), associated with the CDs, was also detected. Visual observation under a UV lamp at 254 nm revealed that the Tb-CDs NAs solutions exhibited a emission color change from red to yellow and eventually to bright green, demonstrating the potential of Tb-CDs NAs for FL-based visual detection of DPA. Additionally, the FL signal of Tb-CDs NAs reached equilibrium within just 1 min and remained stable throughout a 20-min observation period (Fig. S6), indicating a rapid and consistent response. The correlation between the relative emission intensities at characteristic peak positions and DPA concentration was analyzed (Fig. 2b and Fig. S7). To enhance response sensitivity, the peak at 545 nm, which exhibited the largest absolute intensity variation, was selected for most analyses in this study. As shown in Fig. 2c, a strong linear relationship was observed at low DPA concentrations, ranging from 0 to 1.4 μM. The limit of detection of Tb-CDs NAs was calculated to be 0.68 μM, based on the equation 3σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation from three repeated luminescence measurements and k is the slope of calibration curve), which is significantly lower than the infectious dose of spores (60 μM) [6]. The selectivity and anti-interference ability of Tb-CDs NAs, essential properties for an effective FL probe, were subsequently investigated. Potential interferers commonly found in cellular environments, such as NAG, L-Lys, D-Glu, D-Ala, L-Ala, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, K+, Cl⁻, NO3⁻, PO4 3⁻ and structural analogs like o-PA, BA, and 2-PA, were selected for testing. As shown in Fig. 2d and Fig. S8, no significant responses were observed with the addition of these interferers, whereas a strong FL enhancement was only detected in Tb-CDs NAs upon the addition of DPA and the mixture. This was further confirmed by the corresponding photos taken under UV light, demonstrating the high selectivity and strong anti-interference properties of Tb-CDs NAs. These findings at the solution level confirm the feasibility of Tb-CDs NAs as a DPA sensing probe, offering visual detection, a low detection limit, rapid response time, and excellent selectivity.

Building on the promising DPA sensing performance of Tb-CDs NAs in solution, their sensing capabilities were further tested in more complex environments, such as spore suspensions and real samples. The experimental procedures, including spore germination, sample preparation, and detection, are illustrated in Fig. 3a. L-alanine-treated B. subtilis spore suspensions, at varying concentrations and without purification, were directly used for sensing. As shown in Fig. 3b, a spore concentration-dependent increase in FL intensity was observed, along with clear emission color changes under UV light. To validate their detection capability in practical scenarios, bacterial spore-contaminated rice was used as a model. As depicted in Fig. 3c, similar FL responses were detected in the Tb-CDs NAs-added real samples, with significant FL enhancement observed at spore concentrations as low as 1 × 106 spores mL−1. These results suggest the potential application of Tb-CDs NAs for detecting bacterial spores, particularly in real samples, based on the DPA-triggered FL response. Additionally, a control experiment was conducted using rice dispersions alone, which contained components such as starch and sugar, to confirm that the FL signal enhancement was mainly attributed to the presence of bacterial spores. As shown in Fig. S9, a slight enhancement in the FL emission peak around 680 nm was observed with the addition of different volumes of rice dispersions. However, the absence of bacterial spores had a minimal effect on the characteristic FL emission peaks of Tb3+ ions. Thus, the significant increase in FL emission peaks can be attributed to the bacterial spores rather than the rice dispersion.

To explore the potential mechanism behind DPA detection by Tb-CDs NAs, TEM analysis was conducted on Tb-CDs NAs after treatment with DPA. The results clearly indicated that DPA triggered the disassembly of Tb-CDs NAs, evidenced by the disappearance of their striped nanostructures (Fig. 3d). Moreover, according to the FT-IR spectrum of Tb-CDs NAs after incubation with DPA (Fig. S10), an increase in peak intensities at 1400, 1600 and 3200 cm−1 was noted, corresponding to C–N, CO, and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. This provides circumstantial evidence for an increased proportion of free CDs. This disassembly is likely due to the stronger coordination affinity between DPA and Tb3+ ions, which displaced the CDs from the assembly, resulting in the release of CDs and the partial recovery of their FL. This hypothesis was further confirmed by UV-Vis absorption spectrum of DPA-incubated Tb-CDs NAs (Fig. 3e), which showed an increase in the characteristic peaks of free CDs. Additionally, coordination with DPA sensitized the Tb3+ ions, leading to a significant enhancement in FL through antenna effect [31]. Thus, leveraging the FL properties of both CDs and Tb³⁺ ions, a sensitive, visualizable FL probe was successfully developed.

Monitoring the bacterial spore germination process through visualization not only provides insights into the changes occurring in the spores but also aids in determining the optimal timing for effective bacterial inactivation. However, observing FL changes in the lanthanide metal ion channel typically requires deep UV excitation, which is incompatible with conventional fluorescence microscopy. In this study, the CDs channel was utilized for imaging bacterial spore germination using a 405 nm excitation wavelength. Bacillus subtilis spores were germinated for various durations in response to L-alanine stimulation, followed by Tb-CDs NAs treatment and examination using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) ( Fig. 4a). As shown in Fig. 4b, the areas exhibiting red FL (CDs channel) increased as germination time progressed, and nearly all spores in the field of view were illuminated by 30 min, indicating the germination was almost complete. This observation was corroborated by merged images, where the refractive index of the spores significantly decreased post-germination. The ability of Tb-CDs NAs to track the spore germination process at CDs channel can be attributed to two main factors: first, the release of DPA during germination triggered the disassembly of Tb-CDs NAs, resulting in the release of free CDs, which have a higher affinity for spores than their assembled form. Second, the FL of the CDs was partially enhanced after disassembly, as demonstrated in the DPA detection analysis part.

To mitigate the risks associated with secondary contamination, Tb-CDs NAs were engineered to exhibit both sensitive DPA sensing capabilities and in situ antibacterial properties, thereby preventing the spread of highly pathogenic bacteria. Due to their photodynamic nature of CDs, the ROS generation of Tb-CDs NAs under 660 nm laser irradiation was evaluated using 9,10-anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA) as the indicator. As shown in Fig. 5a–d, the typical absorption peaks of ABDA gradually decreased in the presence of CDs and Tb-CDs NAs, with and without DPA treatment, while minimal reduction was observed in ABDA alone when exposed to laser irradiation, even at higher doses (Fig. S11). This demonstrates the effective ROS production in all three cases. Interestingly, DPA-treated Tb-CDs NAs induced greater absorption reduction compared to Tb-CDs NAs alone, similar to the effect observed with CDs-induced ABDA degradation. This suggests that the photodynamic activity of Tb-CDs NAs increases after DPA treatment, likely due to the DPA-triggered release of free CDs. Additionally, a laser dose-dependent study of 1O2 production revealed that higher laser power doses accelerated ABDA degradation rates and improved final degradation efficiency (Fig. S12).

After confirming the excellent photodynamic performance of Tb-CDs NAs, particularly in the presence of DPA, their in situ sterilization effects were thoroughly evaluated. As shown in Figs. 5e and 5f, Tb-CDs NAs demonstrated effective sterilization against germinated B. subtilis spores when exposed to 660 nm laser irradiation (52 mW cm−2). The number of bacterial colonies progressively decreased with increasing concentrations of Tb-CDs NAs and prolonged laser exposure. Notably, a sharp decline in bacterial colonies was observed when the concentration of Tb-CDs NAs reached 600 μg mL−1. Moreover, nearly 100 % antibacterial efficiency was achieved with 800 μg mL−1 Tb-CDs NAs and 30 min of laser irradiation. Additionally, the sterilization effects of Tb3+ ions was evaluated to confirm that the observed sterilization effects were primarily attributed to the CDs component. As illustrated in Fig. S13, the number of bacterial colonies remained essentially unchanged compared to the control group, even with increasing concentrations of Tb3+ ions, indicating their negligible sterilization effect. These findings suggest that Tb-CDs NAs exhibit strong photodynamic inactivation capabilities and hold promise as an in situ antibacterial agent for eliminating bacterial spores resistant to conventional sterilization methods. Finally, the cytotoxicity of CDs, Tb3+ ions, and Tb-CDs NAs against normal Human Immortalized Epidermal cells were evaluated using the Cell Count Kit-8 assay. As shown in Fig. S14, no significant cytotoxic effects were observed for CDs, Tb3+ ions, or Tb-CDs NAs, even at the highest material concentration of 1 mg/mL, indicating their favorable cytocompatibility.

In summary, we successfully developed versatile Tb-CDs NAs with sensitive DPA sensing and robust 1O2 generation capacities, designed for both visual detection and in situ inactivation of bacterial spores. Leveraging the unique optical properties of CDs and Tb3+ ions, alongside the antenna effect of lanthanide ions, Tb-CDs NAs demonstrated highly sensitive DPA detection across different settings, including spore suspensions and real samples, and even effectively monitored bacterial spore germination. Further studies confirmed that the DPA-triggered disassembly of Tb-CDs NAs led to the release of CDs, facilitating in situ spore inactivation through the generation of 1O2. This work advances the development of multifunctional platforms for detecting and simultaneously inactivating pathogens, while helping to prevent bacterial spread and contamination.

Sun Shan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wang Xinxin: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Lin Hengwei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wang Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wang Henggang: Project administration, Data curation. Sun Haoyi: Project administration, Data curation. Cheng Ke: Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Lin Tao: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52173126), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2024M751152 and 2023M731345), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20221203) and Top Talent Support Program for young and middle-aged people of Wuxi Health Committee (BJ2023043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.137296.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Sun received her Ph.D. from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. Following a postdoctoral fellowship at the Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, she is currently an associate professor at Jiangnan University. Her research focuses on designing biofunctional materials for sensing and antibacterial applications.

Hengwei Lin is currently a professor at Jiangnan University. He received his Ph.D. in chemistry from University of Siegen (Germany) and worked as a postdoctoral fellow at Stanford University and University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, respectively. He started his independent academic career at Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences from 2012, and moved to Jiangnan University in 2019. Research in his group mainly focuses on the development and applications of optical functional materials for information security and disease diagnosis.

Peng Wang received his Ph.D. from Fudan University in 2021. After completing a postdoctoral fellowship at Southeast University, he is now an associate chief physician at the Affiliated Hospital of Jiangnan University. His research primarily focuses on the application of functional materials in medicine.
